tluaIndexKeyRejected.tlua(4,21): error TS100039: An index signature parameter type cannot include 'nil'.
tluaIndexKeyRejected.tlua(5,26): error TS100039: An index signature parameter type cannot include 'nil'.
tluaIndexKeyRejected.tlua(6,25): error TS100039: An index signature parameter type cannot include 'nil'.
tluaIndexKeyRejected.tlua(7,21): error TS100039: An index signature parameter type cannot include 'nil'.
tluaIndexKeyRejected.tlua(11,31): error TS100036: An index signature parameter type cannot be a literal type. Consider using a mapped object type instead.
tluaIndexKeyRejected.tlua(12,31): error TS100036: An index signature parameter type cannot be a literal type. Consider using a mapped object type instead.
tluaIndexKeyRejected.tlua(13,32): error TS100036: An index signature parameter type cannot be a literal type. Consider using a mapped object type instead.
tluaIndexKeyRejected.tlua(14,33): error TS100036: An index signature parameter type cannot be a literal type. Consider using a mapped object type instead.
tluaIndexKeyRejected.tlua(19,15): error TS100040: A table key cannot be 'nil'.
tluaIndexKeyRejected.tlua(21,16): error TS100040: A table key cannot be 'nil'.


==== tluaIndexKeyRejected.tlua (10 errors) ====
    // Two rules survive the widening.
    
    // A key must not be nil: `t[nil]` is an error in Lua, not a lookup (100039).
    interface NilKey { [key: nil]: string }
                        ~~~
!!! error TS100039: An index signature parameter type cannot include 'nil'.
    interface MaybeNilKey { [key: string | nil]: string }
                             ~~~
!!! error TS100039: An index signature parameter type cannot include 'nil'.
    interface UnknownKey { [key: unknown]: string }
                            ~~~
!!! error TS100039: An index signature parameter type cannot include 'nil'.
    interface AnyKey { [key: any]: string }
                        ~~~
!!! error TS100039: An index signature parameter type cannot include 'nil'.
    
    // A key must not be a literal: one key is a named property, or a mapped type (100036).
    // This is the half of TypeScript's rule that stays -- the *generic* half is gone.
    interface StringLiteralKey { [key: "a"]: string }
                                  ~~~
!!! error TS100036: An index signature parameter type cannot be a literal type. Consider using a mapped object type instead.
    interface NumberLiteralKey { [key: 1]: string }
                                  ~~~
!!! error TS100036: An index signature parameter type cannot be a literal type. Consider using a mapped object type instead.
    interface BooleanLiteralKey { [key: true]: string }
                                   ~~~
!!! error TS100036: An index signature parameter type cannot be a literal type. Consider using a mapped object type instead.
    interface UnionOfLiteralsKey { [key: "a" | "b"]: string }
                                    ~~~
!!! error TS100036: An index signature parameter type cannot be a literal type. Consider using a mapped object type instead.
    
    // A table key cannot be nil either (100040), and `unknown` may be nil.
    declare k: string;
    local ok = { [k] = 1 };
    local bad = { [nil] = 1 };
                  ~~~~~
!!! error TS100040: A table key cannot be 'nil'.
    declare u: unknown;
    local bad2 = { [u] = 1 };
                   ~~~
!!! error TS100040: A table key cannot be 'nil'.
    
    // Pattern literal keys remain valid: they denote a set of string keys, not one.
    interface PatternKey { [key: `id-${string}`]: number }
    declare p: PatternKey;
    local n: number = p["id-1"];
    
    // A literal that arrives through instantiation widens to its domain rather than
    // keying an index nothing can represent: a template key completed by a type argument
    // becomes a string index, and a literal type argument a string one.
    interface Prefixed<T extends string> { [key: `a${T}`]: number }
    declare pre: Prefixed<"x">;
    local n2: number = pre["ax"];
    type PK = keyof Prefixed<"x">;
    local pk: PK = "anything";
    declare tl: Table<"a", number>;
    local n3: number = tl["b"];
